title: practical english usage - Part 49

49 present      perfect      or  past:   advanced       points    __ For more details, exceptions and notes on American usage. ►    49.6-49.7.

He got 87%.

simple     past    for   details Joe has passed his exam!

present      perfect      for   news Section 5 Past   and   Perfect     Tenses grammar •  49  present perfect or past: advanced points account for you on 19th May.

I am pleased to confirm that Lloyds Bank.. . has opened a Home Loan I have stocked the infirmary cupboard only yesterday.

A 24-year-old soldier has been killed in a road accident last night.

throughout the country on Friday and Saturday.

Police have arrested more than 900 suspected drugs traffickers in raids advertisements, letters and conversations.

Here are some real examples taken from news broadcasts, newspaper articles, news and give the details in the same clause.

brief news items, where space is limited and there is pressure to announce the but not impossible (though learners should avoid them). They  often occur in yesterday, but not J-have-seen him  yesterday. In fact, such structures are unusual expressions of finished time - we  can say I have seen him  or I saw him Grammars usually say that the present perfect cannot be used together with 5 present      perfect      with    past    time    expressions or Did you ever see anything like that before? (   Have you ever seen ... ?) or I always knew I could trust you. (   I've always known ...) and never when they refer to ‘ time up to now’ .

In an informal style, a simple past tense is sometimes possible with always, ever 4 always,        ever     and   never Did Anna phone today? (meaning 'earlier, when the call was expected’ ) - Has Anna phoned today? (meaning 'any time up to now’ ) natural) I saw Jack this week, and he said... (earlier in the week - simple past more more natural) - I haven't seen Jack this week, (the whole week up to now - present perfect thinking of a finished part of that period. Compare: thinking of the whole period up to now. We   prefer the simple past if we  are past tenses are often both possible. We   prefer the present perfect if we  are With definite expressions of 'time up to now' (e.g. today, this week), perfect and 3  today,      this     week,      etc not 1 knew you would help me! (     I Jwve known ...) not But you promised. ..! (     But-you-have-promised-...) not You're older than I thought, (     .. .-tkan-J have-thought!) not It's not as big as I expected, (     ... as I have expected.) false.

We   use a past tense to refer to a belief that has just been shown to be true or 2 expectation         and   reality:    You're      older      than     I thought grammar •  49  present perfect or past: advanced points Anna phoned five minutes ago. (very recent event - simple past) Europe since 1800. (200-year-old event - present perfect) The French revolution has influenced every popular radical movement in things that happened a long time ago. Compare: fact about recent events. But it is possible to use the present perfect to talk about concerned about their present results, so many present perfect sentences are in recent. Recent events are more likely to be ‘ news’ , and we  are more likely to be The  choice also does not depend directly on whether actions and events are 10 bad   rules    (3): 'recent     actions' I ate the last of the eggs this morning, (finished action - simple past) That cat has eaten your supper, (finished action - present perfect) about finished time periods). Compare: grammars sometimes suggest (though it can depend on whether we  are talking not depend on whether we  are talking about finished actions, as learners’ Note also that the choice between simple present perfect and simple past does 9 bad   rules    (2): 'finished      actions' forest, (simple past with very indefinite time-reference) Once upon a time a little girl lived with her mother in a lonely house in a dark perfect with very definite time-reference) I've lived here for exactly three years, seven months and two days, (present common with definite time expressions. Compare: perfect is not often used with finished time expressions, but it actually is very with expressions referring to ‘ definite time’ . This is conftising - the present Grammars sometimes say that the simple past, not the present perfect, is used 8  bad   rules    (1): 'definite      time' For more about tenses with just, ►    303.
